explanation: The requirement says the robot should do prop_0 and prop_1. This means that both should happen, but it does not specify a particular order, only that eventually both prop_0 and prop_1 must occur at some point in the execution. In LTL, 'eventually prop_0' is F prop_0, and 'eventually prop_1' is F prop_1, and both must hold, so we use the conjunction.

The requirement says that the robot should "prop_0 and prop_1". This can be interpreted as both propositions must always be true, i.e., globally, both hold. In LTL, this is G (prop_0 & prop_1), which means at all times, prop_0 and prop_1 must be true.
The requirement is that the robot should fulfill both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, this means that both atomic propositions must always be true globally.
The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 should occur (no temporal constraint is specified), so the formula expresses that both must eventually be satisfied at some point in the future.
The requirement says the robot should achieve both prop_0 and prop_1. This can be interpreted as: both must eventually happen (eventually prop_0 and eventually prop_1).
The requirement states that the robot should 'prop_0 and prop_1', which we interpret as both prop_0 and prop_1 must be true at every moment. In LTL, this means globally, prop_0 and prop_1 must hold.
The requirement states that the robot should satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, this can be interpreted as both prop_0 and prop_1 are always true globally.

The requirement says the robot should achieve both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, this is interpreted as: 'eventually prop_0 and eventually prop_1' — i.e., F prop_0 & F prop_1. This ensures both must occur (possibly at different times) during execution.

The requirement states that the robot should satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1 globally (i.e., at all times). In LTL, 'should' is most commonly interpreted as a global constraint. Therefore, we use the 'G' (globally) operator with the conjunction of the atomic propositions.

The requirement asks that both prop_0 and prop_1 should hold. Assuming the requirement means they should both always be true, we use globally (G) with conjunction. If it means 'eventually', then F(prop_0 & prop_1) would be used, but typically 'should' (without time) is interpreted as an invariance.
